subject: Tort Law
lesson_title: Rationale, Employment Relationship, Course of Employment, and the Close Connection Test

questions.question: Vicarious liability is best defined as:

options.A: The doctrine by which one person is held legally responsible for the tortious acts of another, even without personal fault
options.B: The doctrine making all persons strictly liable for harm caused by their employees
options.C: The principle that an employer is liable only where they personally directed the tortious act
options.D: The rule that liability for torts is always personal and cannot attach to third parties

questions.explanation: Vicarious liability imposes liability on one person — typically an employer — for the tortious acts of another — typically an employee — even though the employer was not personally at fault. It is a significant departure from the general principle that tort liability is personal.

questions.question: Which UK Supreme Court case, decided in 2012, articulated four rationales justifying the imposition of vicarious liability?

options.A: Lister v Hesley Hall Limited [2002]
options.B: Catholic Child Welfare Society v Various Claimants [2012]
options.C: Cox v Ministry of Justice [2016]
options.D: Barry Congregation of Jehovah's Witnesses v BXB [2023]

questions.explanation: Catholic Child Welfare Society v Various Claimants [2012] UK Supreme Court articulated four rationales for vicarious liability: (1) compensation — employer has means to pay; (2) creation of risk; (3) economic benefit; and (4) incentive to manage risk.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the four rationales for vicarious liability identified in Catholic Child Welfare Society v Various Claimants [2012]?

options.A: The employer created the risk of the tort by employing the individual
options.B: The employer benefits economically from the employee's activities
options.C: The employer has superior knowledge of the tortious conduct than the victim
options.D: The doctrine incentivises employers to ensure careful behaviour

questions.explanation: The four rationales in Catholic Child Welfare Society are: (1) compensation — employer has means to pay; (2) creation of risk; (3) economic benefit; and (4) incentive to manage risk. Superior knowledge of the tortious conduct is not one of the rationales.

questions.question: For a successful vicarious liability claim, which two elements must be established?

options.A: That the employer was negligent and that the employee caused damage
options.B: That the employee was authorised to perform the act and that damage resulted
options.C: That the employer had insurance and that the employee intended the harm
options.D: That there is a relevant relationship between the defendant and the tortfeasor, and that the tort was committed in the course of that relationship

questions.explanation: Two elements must be established for vicarious liability: (1) a relevant relationship between the defendant and the tortfeasor — typically employment or akin to employment; and (2) the tortious act was committed in the course of that relationship.

questions.question: The earliest test for distinguishing an employee from an independent contractor was the control test. What does this test ask?

options.A: Whether the alleged employer has the right to direct not only what the person does, but also how they do it
options.B: Whether the person is integrated into the economic structure of the employer's business
options.C: Whether the person provides their own equipment and bears their own financial risk
options.D: Whether the parties have entered into a formal written contract of employment

questions.explanation: The control test asks whether the alleged employer has the right to direct not only what the person does, but also how they do it. The greater the degree of control, the more likely the relationship is one of employment. However, it proved insufficient for skilled modern occupations.

questions.question: The integration test, associated with Lord Denning, asks whether:

options.A: The employer controls the manner in which the person performs their work
options.B: The person's work is integrated into the organisation — forming part of the business — or provided to it from the outside
options.C: The person receives a salary or a fee for their services
options.D: The person can simultaneously work for other employers

questions.explanation: The integration test shifts the question from control to organisational integration: is the person's work integrated into the organisation — are they part of the business — or do they provide services to it from the outside? A surgeon employed by a hospital is integrated; a visiting consultant is not.

questions.question: The economic reality (or multiple factors) test for employment status considers which of the following?

options.A: Only the label used by the parties in their written contract
options.B: Only the degree of control exercised by the alleged employer
options.C: The totality of the parties' relationship — including equipment provision, ability to work for others, financial risk, method of payment, and the parties' own description
options.D: Only whether the person paid PAYE taxes through the alleged employer's payroll

questions.explanation: The economic reality test considers the totality of the relationship. Relevant factors include: provision of own equipment, ability to work for others, degree of financial risk, salary vs fee payment, and the parties' own description of the relationship — though this is not conclusive.

questions.question: In O'Keeffe v Hickey [2008], the Irish Supreme Court held that the State was not vicariously liable for the sexual abuse of a pupil by a national school teacher. The reason was:

options.A: The teacher was acting outside the course of their employment when the abuse occurred
options.B: The close connection test had not yet been adopted in Irish law
options.C: The plaintiff had not established the necessary proximity of relationship
options.D: National school teachers were not employees of the State — they were under the management and control of the school patron (the Church), not the State

questions.explanation: In O'Keeffe v Hickey [2008], the Supreme Court found the State was not vicariously liable because national school teachers were not employees of the State. The complex arrangement between the Church and State meant the teacher was under the management of the school patron — the Church — not the State.

questions.question: Catholic Child Welfare Society v Various Claimants [2012] recognised that vicarious liability could arise in relationships 'akin to employment'. What two-stage framework did the Court use?

options.A: First, whether the relationship is capable of giving rise to vicarious liability; second, whether the tort was committed in the course of that relationship
options.B: First, whether the employer had insurance; second, whether the tort was foreseeable
options.C: First, whether the tortfeasor was integrated into the business; second, whether the employer authorised the act
options.D: First, whether the relationship involved a written contract; second, whether PAYE was paid

questions.explanation: Catholic Child Welfare Society established a two-stage framework for relationships akin to employment: (1) whether the relationship is capable of giving rise to vicarious liability; and (2) whether the tort was committed in the course of that relationship. This was refined in Barry Congregation [2023].

questions.question: In Cox v Ministry of Justice [2016], the UK Supreme Court found the Prison Service vicariously liable for a prisoner's negligence in the prison kitchen. This case established that:

options.A: The Prison Service was an employer of prisoners under the statutory definition
options.B: Vicarious liability can arise in relationships akin to employment — here, the Prison Service directed and controlled the prisoner's work and benefited from it
options.C: The prisoner was an employee because the Prison Service paid them a wage
options.D: Vicarious liability for prisoners' acts requires specific statutory authorisation

questions.explanation: Cox v Ministry of Justice [2016] extended vicarious liability to relationships akin to employment. Although prisoners are not employees, the Prison Service directed and controlled the prisoner's work and the prisoner's labour was integrated into and benefited its operations — making the relationship sufficiently akin to employment.

questions.question: The Salmond test for 'course of employment' provides that an employee acts within the course of employment when they do:

options.A: Anything that occurs during their contracted working hours
options.B: Anything the employer could have foreseen happening on the job
options.C: Something expressly authorised by the employer, or an unauthorised but wrongful mode of doing something that was authorised
options.D: Anything connected to their employment, even if done off-premises and off-hours

questions.explanation: The Salmond test provides that an employee acts in the course of employment when doing: (1) something expressly authorised by the employer; or (2) something not authorised, but which is a wrongful or unauthorised mode of doing something that was authorised. A negligently driven delivery vehicle is the classic example.

questions.question: Why does the Salmond test become strained in cases of deliberate assault or sexual abuse by employees?

options.A: Because the Salmond test was designed only for road traffic accident cases
options.B: Because employers are strictly liable for all intentional torts under statute
options.C: Because deliberate assault requires application of the criminal law rather than tort law
options.D: Because an assault can never be characterised as an unauthorised mode of an authorised act — no employer authorises abuse

questions.explanation: The Salmond test breaks down for intentional wrongdoing because deliberate abuse cannot be characterised as a 'wrongful mode' of an authorised act — no employer ever authorises sexual abuse. This was the problem that Lister v Hesley Hall [2002] addressed with the close connection test.

questions.question: Lister and Others v Hesley Hall Limited [2002] introduced which test for vicarious liability in cases of intentional wrongdoing?

options.A: The close connection test — whether there is a sufficiently close connection between the employment and the wrongful act to make it just to impose vicarious liability
options.B: The foreseeability test — whether the employer could foresee the risk of the employee committing the harm
options.C: The authorisation test — whether the employer expressly or impliedly authorised the wrongful act
options.D: The control test — whether the employer directed the manner in which the wrongful act was performed

questions.explanation: Lister v Hesley Hall [2002] introduced the close connection test. Lord Steyn held that the question is whether there is a sufficiently close connection between the employment and the wrongful act to make it just and reasonable to impose vicarious liability on the employer.

questions.question: On the facts of Lister v Hesley Hall [2002], why was the close connection test satisfied?

options.A: Because the employer had expressly authorised the warden to have physical contact with the boys
options.B: Because the warden's duty was to care for the boys in the residential house, and the abuse occurred in the precise context of that duty — creating an intimate connection with the employment
options.C: Because the employer had failed to conduct a criminal background check on the warden
options.D: Because the abuse occurred on the employer's premises during working hours

questions.explanation: In Lister, the warden's employment duty was to care for the boys in the residential house. The sexual abuse occurred in that precise caring context — it was intimately connected with the employment. The close connection between the employment and the abuse was sufficient for vicarious liability.

questions.question: Hickey v McGowan [2017] is significant in Irish tort law because it:

options.A: First recognised the doctrine of vicarious liability in Irish law
options.B: Held that the State was vicariously liable for abuse by national school teachers
options.C: Confirmed the close connection test in Irish law and held a religious order vicariously liable for abuse by a Christian Brother teacher
options.D: Abolished the Salmond test in Ireland and replaced it entirely with the close connection test

questions.explanation: Hickey v McGowan [2017] is the leading Irish Supreme Court authority on the close connection test. Clarke J confirmed the test's application in Irish law and found the religious order vicariously liable — the Brother's position of trust and authority over children created a sufficiently close connection with the abuse.

questions.question: On the facts of Hickey v McGowan [2017], what was the basis for finding the religious order vicariously liable?

options.A: The religious order had failed to conduct a criminal background check on the Brother
options.B: The abuse occurred on the school's premises during school hours
options.C: The Brother had been expressly authorised to be alone with pupils
options.D: The Brother's position as a teacher placed in authority over children created a sufficiently close connection with the abuse

questions.explanation: In Hickey v McGowan [2017], the religious order was vicariously liable because the Brother's position as a teacher — placed in trust and authority over children — created a sufficiently close connection between that employment relationship and the abusive acts.

questions.question: Barry Congregation of Jehovah's Witnesses v BXB [2023] refined the vicarious liability framework by confirming:

options.A: A two-stage test: (1) whether the relationship is one of employment or akin to employment; and (2) whether there is a sufficient connection between that relationship and the tortious act
options.B: That religious organisations are always vicariously liable for acts of their ministers
options.C: That the close connection test no longer applies to cases involving deliberate wrongdoing
options.D: That only a formal employment contract can give rise to vicarious liability

questions.explanation: Barry Congregation of Jehovah's Witnesses v BXB [2023] UK Supreme Court consolidated and refined the two-stage test: (1) whether the relationship between defendant and tortfeasor is one of employment or akin to employment; and (2) whether there is a sufficient connection between that relationship and the tortious act.

questions.question: A delivery driver employed by a courier company is told to use only approved routes. The driver takes a different, shorter route and negligently causes an accident. Is the employer vicariously liable?

options.A: No — the driver was acting outside the scope of employment by using an unauthorised route
options.B: Yes — negligent driving is an unauthorised mode of doing the authorised act of driving, satisfying the Salmond test
options.C: Only if the employer was aware the driver regularly used alternative routes
options.D: Only if the accident occurred within the driver's normal working hours

questions.explanation: Under the Salmond test, an employee acts within the course of employment when doing something that is an unauthorised but wrongful mode of doing what was authorised. Driving negligently — even on an unapproved route — is a wrongful mode of the authorised act of driving. The employer is vicariously liable.

questions.question: A security guard, employed to protect a nightclub, assaults a patron in anger after an argument. Under which test should a court assess vicarious liability of the nightclub owner?

options.A: The Salmond test only — the assault is a wrongful mode of the authorised activity of securing the venue
options.B: The control test — because the nightclub exercised control over the guard's actions
options.C: The close connection test from Lister v Hesley Hall — was there a sufficiently close connection between the employment and the wrongful act?
options.D: The integration test — was the guard integrated into the nightclub's business structure?

questions.explanation: Cases of deliberate assault go beyond what the Salmond test can accommodate. The close connection test from Lister v Hesley Hall [2002] — as confirmed in Hickey v McGowan [2017] — applies: was there a sufficiently close connection between the employment and the wrongful act to make it just to impose vicarious liability?

questions.question: At common law, an employer is generally NOT vicariously liable for the acts of which category of person?

options.A: A part-time employee
options.B: An employee on a zero-hours contract
options.C: A temporary employee placed by an employment agency
options.D: An independent contractor

questions.explanation: At common law, an employer is generally not vicariously liable for the acts of an independent contractor. This distinction — between employees and independent contractors — is therefore central to vicarious liability analysis, determined by the control, integration, and economic reality tests.

questions.question: Why did the control test prove insufficient as the sole test for employment status in modern contexts?

options.A: Because in skilled professional occupations — surgeons, engineers, software developers — the employer often cannot dictate how the work is performed
options.B: Because the control test was found to be discriminatory against self-employed workers
options.C: Because the control test was overruled by statute in Ireland in 2003
options.D: Because only the economic reality test is now recognised by Irish courts

questions.explanation: The control test proved insufficient because in modern professional and skilled occupations — surgeons, engineers, software developers — an employer typically cannot and does not dictate how the work is performed. The absence of control does not mean there is no employment relationship.

questions.question: O'Keeffe v Hickey [2008] established which important principle for determining employment status in Irish law?

options.A: That religious organisations are never vicariously liable for the acts of clergy
options.B: That employment status requires careful analysis of the actual legal and factual relationship, not merely its label
options.C: That the State is always vicariously liable for abuse occurring in State-funded schools
options.D: That vicarious liability does not apply to cases of intentional abuse

questions.explanation: O'Keeffe v Hickey [2008] is important for the proposition that employment status in Irish law requires careful analysis of the actual legal and factual relationship — not the label used by the parties. The Supreme Court found that national school teachers were not employees of the State despite State funding.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly states the FE-1 exam approach to a vicarious liability problem question?

options.A: Identify whether the employer had insurance, then apply strict liability principles
options.B: Focus only on whether the employer authorised the tortious act
options.C: First establish the relevant relationship (employment or akin to employment); then determine whether the tort was committed in the course of that relationship, applying Salmond for negligent acts and the close connection test for intentional wrongdoing
options.D: Apply the Salmond test universally to all types of tortious act committed by employees

questions.explanation: The FE-1 approach requires: (1) establishing the relevant relationship using control/integration/economic reality tests; (2) determining course of employment — using Salmond for negligent acts and the Lister/Hickey close connection test for intentional wrongdoing. Always cite Hickey v McGowan as the leading Irish authority.

questions.question: An employee who is a skilled electrician works for a contractor. The contractor sends the electrician to a client's premises to install wiring. The electrician negligently causes a fire. Which test is most appropriate to determine the electrician's employment status?

options.A: The control test alone — the contractor must show it directed how the electrician wired the circuits
options.B: The integration test alone — whether the electrician was integrated into the contractor's organisational structure
options.C: The authorisation test — whether the contractor expressly permitted the electrician to work on the client's premises
options.D: The economic reality (multiple factors) test — considering provision of equipment, financial risk, ability to work for others, and method of payment

questions.explanation: For a skilled professional like an electrician, the control test alone is inadequate as the employer typically cannot direct how the technical work is performed. The economic reality (multiple factors) test is the most appropriate — considering all aspects of the relationship including equipment, financial risk, and payment method.

questions.question: The close connection test, introduced in Lister v Hesley Hall [2002] and confirmed in Ireland in Hickey v McGowan [2017], asks:

options.A: Whether there is a sufficiently close connection between the employment and the wrongful act to make it just and reasonable to impose vicarious liability on the employer
options.B: Whether the employer expressly or impliedly knew the employee might commit the wrongful act
options.C: Whether the wrongful act was committed on the employer's premises during working hours
options.D: Whether the employer derived financial profit from the specific activity during which the tort was committed

questions.explanation: The close connection test asks whether there is a sufficiently close connection between the employment and the wrongful act to make it just and reasonable to impose vicarious liability. It is the leading test for intentional wrongdoing and was confirmed in Irish law by Hickey v McGowan [2017].

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the relationship between the Salmond test and the close connection test?

options.A: They are mutually exclusive — if the Salmond test applies, the close connection test cannot
options.B: The Salmond test governs negligent acts; the close connection test applies as a supplement or replacement for intentional wrongdoing where the Salmond test is strained
options.C: The close connection test has completely replaced the Salmond test in all contexts
options.D: The Salmond test applies in Ireland; the close connection test is limited to English law

questions.explanation: The Salmond test works well for negligent acts (unauthorised modes of authorised acts). The close connection test supplements or replaces it where the Salmond test is strained — primarily cases of deliberate/intentional wrongdoing such as assault and abuse.

questions.question: Cox v Ministry of Justice [2016] is significant for vicarious liability because it demonstrates that:

options.A: The State is vicariously liable for all acts committed by persons in its institutions
options.B: Prisoners can bring personal injury claims in tort against the Prison Service
options.C: Vicarious liability can arise in relationships akin to employment, even where there is no formal employment contract — the key factors are direction, control, and integration into operations
options.D: The Salmond test applies to determine course of employment in all institutional settings

questions.explanation: Cox v Ministry of Justice [2016] demonstrates that vicarious liability extends to relationships akin to employment. A prisoner working in a prison kitchen — directed and controlled by the Prison Service, with their labour integrated into its operations — created such a relationship despite the absence of a contract of employment.

questions.question: A hospital employs a surgeon under a formal employment contract. The surgeon performs an operation negligently, injuring a patient. Is the hospital vicariously liable?

options.A: No — surgeons are independent professionals and cannot be employees of a hospital
options.B: No — vicarious liability does not apply to professional negligence claims
options.C: Yes — only if the hospital specifically directed the surgical technique used
options.D: Yes — the surgeon is an employee, and the negligent surgery was an unauthorised mode of performing the authorised task of operating on patients

questions.explanation: A hospital surgeon employed under a formal contract is an employee. Negligent surgery is an unauthorised (wrongful) mode of performing the authorised act of operating on patients — satisfying the Salmond test. The hospital is vicariously liable.

questions.question: For the FE-1, when a problem question involves vicarious liability for intentional wrongdoing, which is the leading Irish authority to cite?

options.A: Hickey v McGowan [2017] — the leading Irish Supreme Court authority on the close connection test in cases of intentional wrongdoing
options.B: O'Keeffe v Hickey [2008] — the leading Irish case on vicarious liability for institutional abuse
options.C: Connolly v Dundalk UDC [1990] — confirming that the non-delegable duty applies to intentional torts
options.D: Catholic Child Welfare Society v Various Claimants [2012] — the only applicable authority in Irish law

questions.explanation: Hickey v McGowan [2017] is the leading Irish Supreme Court authority on the close connection test and vicarious liability for intentional wrongdoing. It confirmed the test's application in Irish law and is the primary case to cite in FE-1 answers on this topic.

questions.question: An employee drives their own car to meet a client during working hours, deviates to run a personal errand, and negligently causes an accident during the detour. Under the Salmond test, is the employer vicariously liable for the accident during the detour?

options.A: Yes — the accident occurred during working hours so it is within the course of employment
options.B: No — the detour for a personal errand is a 'frolic of their own', taking the employee outside the course of employment
options.C: Yes — driving to meet a client is an authorised act, and any negligent driving is within the course of employment
options.D: Only if the employer had expressly prohibited personal use of the employee's own vehicle

questions.explanation: Under the Salmond test, a substantial deviation from the authorised route or purpose — a 'frolic of their own' — takes the employee outside the course of employment. Running a personal errand during a work journey constitutes such a frolic, and the employer would not be vicariously liable for the accident during that detour.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about vicarious liability best reflects the current state of Irish and UK law?

options.A: Vicarious liability is limited to classic employment relationships and cannot extend to volunteers or prisoners
options.B: Vicarious liability arises only where the employer authorised the tortious act, either expressly or impliedly
options.C: Vicarious liability can arise in relationships akin to employment; for intentional wrongdoing, the close connection test applies; and the doctrine is founded on rationales of compensation, risk creation, economic benefit, and incentive
options.D: The Salmond test is the sole test for course of employment in all categories of vicarious liability claim

questions.explanation: The current state of the law, reflecting Catholic Child Welfare Society [2012], Lister [2002], Hickey v McGowan [2017], and Cox [2016], is that: (1) vicarious liability extends to relationships akin to employment; (2) the close connection test applies to intentional wrongdoing; and (3) the doctrine is underpinned by the four rationales.
